                                          Board of Education Agenda
                                               March 24, 2026


                               PUBLIC PARTICIPATION GUIDELINES
Board of Education meetings are held in person in the board room located at 3738 Walnut Avenue, Carmichael,
California. Alternatively, you can view the board meeting virtually via livestream from a computer, mobile device
or tablet at https://www.sanjuan.edu/boardmeeting.

The full agenda packet is available on the district website at https://www.sanjuan.edu/boardagendas. The district
has taken the following steps to assist the public in offering public comment:

     1. In Person Public Comment. Public comment may be offered in person during the board meeting at the
        district office located at 3738 Walnut Avenue, Carmichael, California. Board Bylaw 9323 limits visitor
        comments to two (2) minutes per speaker, with no more than 30 minutes per single topic. Time will be
        extended for any speaker who uses an interpreter. Please be aware that public comments, including your
        name, become part of the public record.
     2. Online Submission of Public Comment. Members of the public may submit written comments by using the
        comment form located on the district website at https://www.sanjuan.edu/boardmeeting. If you wish to
        submit a written comment on more than one agenda item, please submit a separate form for each item on
        which you are commenting. Written comments are limited to 1,500 characters. Comments will be provided
        to the members of the board.

            Action: The superintendent is recommending that the board adopt Resolution No. A-464, proclaiming

                                               Mission Statement
We partner with our San Juan Unified community in setting high expectations for academic achievement rooted in
equity and unity to create supportive learning experiences in which all students, families and staff can excel and
thrive.

                                    San Juan Unified Land Acknowledgement
The San Juan Unified School District acknowledges that our schools are located on the ancestral lands of the
Nisenan and Miwok peoples, who continue to care for this land as they have since time immemorial. We deeply
respect their ongoing connection to this land and recognize their resilience in preserving and revitalizing their
cultures and traditions despite historical and ongoing challenges.

We recognize the profound injustices endured by their ancestors, including genocide, forced assimilation, and
displacement, and we remain aware of the ongoing challenges and injustices they face today. As we advance in our
commitment to equity and inclusion, we pledge to strengthen our connections by fostering a culture of
understanding, respect, and active collaboration with their communities.

                                             Board of Education Minutes
                                                  March 10, 2026

Regular Meeting
Board of Education
5:30 p.m.

Open Session/Call to Order (A)
At 5:30 p.m., the March 10 regular meeting was called to order by the vice president, Manuel Perez. The board meeting
was held in person and was also livestreamed on the district website.

Roll Call
Present:
Manuel Perez, vice president
Nick Bloise, clerk
Zima Creason, member
Abid Stanekzai, member
Pam Costa, member
Ben Avey, member
Absent:
Tanya Kravchuk, president

Announcement of Closed Session Topics/Closed Session Visitor Comments (A-1)
There were no closed session visitor comments.

Closed Session (B)
The meeting was then recessed with the board convening in closed session to discuss one personnel matter (Government
Code section 54957) – non-reelection of probationary teachers (Education Code section 44929.21[b]); to discuss collective
bargaining matters – discussion with negotiator Trent Allen, Chief of Staff, regarding CSEA Chapter 127
General/Operations support, Chauffeurs/Teamsters Local No. 150 Transportation, Supervisors, Teachers and Certificated
Supervisory units, and regarding non-represented groups: management and confidential units (Government Code section
54957.6); and to conference with legal counsel - anticipated litigation – social media (Government Code section
54956.9(d)(2) and (4)).

Reconvene Open Session/Pledge of Allegiance (C)
At 6:30 p.m., the March 10 regular meeting was called back to order by the vice president, Manuel Perez. Four members
of the Casa Roble Fundamental High School Jr. ROTC led the group in the Pledge of Allegiance.

Minutes Approved (D)
It was moved by Mr. Stanekzai, seconded by Ms. Creason, that the minutes of the February 24 regular meeting be approved.
MOTION CARRIED 5-0-1-1 [AYES: Bloise, Creason, Stanekzai, Costa, Avey; NOES: None; ABSENT: Kravchuk;
ABSTAIN: Perez].

Recognition: Classified Employees of the Year (E-1)
Senior Director of Human Resources Cloris Henry, Ed.D., introduced the nine 2026 Classified Employees of the Year:
Joanne McLaughlin Strauch, Secretary, Earl LeGette Elementary School (Clerical and Administrative Services); Mikalai
Lutsyk, Head Custodian, Grand Oaks Elementary School (Custodian and Maintenance Services); Debbie Armstrong,

                                                                                                                    2745
                                                                                                                            5
Nutrition Services Cook, Del Campo High School (Nutrition Services); Jamileh Piran, Health Instructional Assistant,
Health Services (Health and Student Services); Cortney Lloret, Instructional Assistant, Alternative Learning Center -
Meraki High School (Paraprofessional Services); Omar Marroquin, School Community Specialist, Encina High School
(Security Services); Peter McGuinness, Lead Electrician, Maintenance and Operations (Skilled Trade Services); William
Dreifus, Technology Support Specialist II, Technology Services (Technical Services); and Joseph Lausmann,
Transportation Operations Technician, Transportation (Transportation Services).

High School Student Council Reports (F-1)
High School Student Council representatives Emi Kuwabara and Katie Sue Amerine from El Camino Fundamental High
School and Ojehi Benito-Oserogho and Bidaa Saleh from Encina High School updated the board on the goals, activities
and achievements at their respective schools.

Board-appointed/District Committees (F-2)
Mariya Babiychuk, chair of the Curriculum, Standards, Instructional and Student Services Committee (C&S), reported that
the committee voted unanimously to recommend approval of the proposed list of new high school courses for 2026-2027.

Closed Session/Expulsion Actions (F-6)
Mr. Bloise reported that the board voted unanimously to non-reelect seven probationary teachers, identified by employee
numbers 583753, 581749, 587414, 588403, 538863, 588585 and 53504. Mr. Bloise also reported that no action was taken
on item B-3 regarding conference with legal counsel - anticipated litigation – social media (Government Code section
54956.9(d)(2) and (4)).

Visitor Comments (G)
Jason Brown spoke about his son’s experiences at El Camino Fundamental High School.

Karen VanWinkle expressed support for constructing a performing arts center at Bella Vista High School.

Consent Calendar Approved (H-2/H-7)
Superintendent Bassanelli pulled item H-1. Mr. Perez pulled item H-8. It was moved by Mr. Bloise, seconded by Ms.
Creason, that the consent calendar items H-2 through H-7 be approved. MOTION CARRIED 6-0-1 [AYES: Perez, Bloise,
Creason, Stanekzai, Costa, Avey; NOES: None; ABSENT: Kravchuk].

Purchasing Report (H-2)
Purchase orders, service agreements and contracts; change orders/amendments; construction and public works bids and
contracts; and piggyback contracts – approved as submitted.

Surplus Report (H-3)
Approval to dispose of surplus property pursuant to Board Policy 3270.

Resolution No. 4253: School Board Election Order (H-4)
Adoption of Resolution No. 4253 certifying the election order for November 3, 2026, for electing four governing board
members (trustee area no. 3, trustee area no. 5, trustee area no. 6, trustee area no. 7), limiting qualification statements to 200
words, requiring individual candidates to pre-pay all costs associated with providing the qualification statements,
determining the winner(s) by lot in case of a tie vote and requesting the school board election be consolidated with the
general election.

Mira Loma High School Locker Rooms Improvement Project CEQA Notice of Exemption and Categorical
Exemption (H-5)
Approval of the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Notice of Exemption and Categorical Exemption for the
Mira Loma High School locker rooms improvement project.

Resolution No. 4256: Sixth Dimension PMCM Inc. Master Contract Agreement for Construction Management
Services Amendment No.1 (H-6)
Adoption of Resolution No. 4256, approving the first amendment to the Master Contract Agreement for Construction
Management Services between San Juan Unified School District and Sixth Dimension PMCM Inc.



                                                                                                                            2746
                                                                                                                                     6
Resolution No. 4257: Amendment No. 3 for the Gateway International School (Winterstein Site) Portable, Field
Modification, TK/Kindergarten Wing and Shade Structure Project (H-7)
Adoption of Resolution No. 4257, approving Amendment No. 3 to the Facilities Lease to include the revised Lease Payment
Schedule and District Contingency as outlined in Amendment No. 3.

Consent Calendar Continued (I)
Personnel Report (H-1) - Appointments, leaves of absence and separations.
Superintendent Bassanelli explained that the item was pulled in order to remove dismissal CL-663 from the list of
separations in the personnel report.

It was moved by Mr. Avey, seconded by Mr. Stanekzai, that the consent calendar item H-1 be approved as amended.
MOTION CARRIED 6-0-1 [AYES: Perez, Bloise, Creason, Stanekzai, Costa, Avey; NOES: None; ABSENT: Kravchuk].

Certification of Absence: Manuel Perez (H-8)
Certification that the February 24, 2026, absence of Board Member Manuel Perez occurred due to illness, pursuant to
Education Code 35120(c) and Board Bylaw 9250.

It was moved by Mr. Bloise, seconded by Mr. Stanekzai, that the consent calendar item H-8 be approved. MOTION
CARRIED 5-0-1-1 [AYES: Bloise, Creason, Stanekzai, Costa, Avey; NOES: None; ABSENT: Kravchuk; ABSTAIN:
Perez].

New High School Courses for 2026-2027 (J-1)
Deputy Superintendent of Schools and Student Support Timothy Chip Dale, Ed.D., presented the topic and then introduced
Director of Professional Learning and Curriculum Innovation Nicole Kukral. Ms. Kukral explained the new course request
process, discussed community feedback themes and reviewed the list of newly proposed high school courses for 2026-2027
which include the following: Arts: Commercial Music/Modern Band, Jazz Choir, Mariachi Ensemble, Musical Theater, and
Percussion Ensemble; Electives: Personal Finance (a new California graduation requirement, effective for the class of 2030-
2031, which must be offered by 2027-2028), Philosophy, and Project Management.

Board members made comments. Mr. Avey expressed support for the proposed courses, highlighting Project Management
and Personal Finance. Mr. Avey noted that as more courses are added, it becomes increasingly challenging for students to
fit everything into their schedules, raising questions about block versus traditional schedules and ensuring students still
have access to a range of elective options. Ms. Creason also supported the proposed courses, emphasizing the Personal
Finance course and the importance of connecting coursework to careers. Ms. Creason also encouraged a focus on starting
with job pathways and then helping students understand transferable skills and career relevance. Ms. Costa reported that
the C&S committee voted unanimously to recommend the approval of the proposed courses, noting the thoughtful
discussion at the committee meeting and sharing that teachers and administrators expressed enthusiasm for the courses.
Action was scheduled for March 24.

Budget Financial Status Report / 2025-2026 Second Interim Report (J-2)
Chief Financial Officer Joel Ryan presented the 2025-2026 Second Interim Report, certifying the Second Interim Report
as positive, with the understanding of the fiduciary responsibility to maintain fiscal solvency for the current and subsequent
two fiscal years. The Second Interim Report reflects actual revenue and expenditures through January 31, 2026, forecasts
the remainder of the 2025-2026 fiscal year, and provides a multi-year budget projection for years 2026-2027 and 2027-
2028.

It was moved by Ms. Costa, seconded by Mr. Bloise, to approve the 2025-2026 Second Interim Report with the positive
certification of the district’s ability to meet its financial obligations for the current fiscal year and subsequent two fiscal
years. MOTION CARRIED 6-0-1 [AYES: Perez, Bloise, Creason, Stanekzai, Costa, Avey; NOES: None; ABSENT:
Kravchuk].

Public Hearing No. 2: Aspire Alexander Twilight Secondary Academy Charter School Renewal Petition (J-3)
Assistant Superintendent of Educational Services Harvey Oaxaca, Ed.D., presented the item explaining that the district is
recommending a five-year renewal for the charter school. Mr. Perez declared the topic of the Aspire Alexander Twilight
Secondary Academy charter school renewal petition a public hearing and invited the public to speak. There being no
comments from the public, Mr. Perez declared the public hearing closed.


                                                                                                                         2747
                                                                                                                                  7
It was moved by Mr. Bloise, seconded by Mr. Avey, to approve the Aspire Alexander Twilight Secondary Academy charter
school renewal petition for a term of five years starting July 1, 2026, through June 30, 2031. MOTION CARRIED 6-0-1
[AYES: Perez, Bloise, Creason, Stanekzai, Costa, Avey; NOES: None; ABSENT: Kravchuk].

Public Hearing No. 2: Aspire Alexander Twilight College Preparatory Academy Charter School Renewal Petition (J-4)
Assistant Superintendent of Educational Services Harvey Oaxaca, Ed.D., presented the item explaining that Aspire
Alexander Twilight College Preparatory Academy, pursuant to Education Code section 47607.2, meets the criteria for low-
performing charter school status and is therefore only eligible for a two-year renewal. Dr. Oaxaca also explained that the
charter school has developed and approved a performance improvement plan to monitor student progress and improve
academic performance outcomes. Mr. Perez declared the topic of the Aspire Alexander Twilight College Preparatory
Academy charter school renewal petition a public hearing and invited the public to speak. There being no comments from
the public, Mr. Perez declared the public hearing closed.

It was moved by Mr. Avey, seconded by Mr. Bloise, to approve the Aspire Alexander Twilight College Preparatory
Academy charter school renewal petition for a term of two years starting July 1, 2026, through June 30, 2028. MOTION
CARRIED 6-0-1 [AYES: Perez, Bloise, Creason, Stanekzai, Costa, Avey; NOES: None; ABSENT: Kravchuk].

Board Reports (K)
Ms. Costa reported that she and her granddaughter attended the esports tournament at Barrett Middle School, where students
enjoyed the event, and she thanked the hosting departments.

Mr. Avey reported that he attended a lacrosse booster club bingo fundraiser at Bella Vista High School, noting the work of
volunteers who support extracurricular activities. Mr. Avey also shared that he visited Gold River Discovery Center,
provided an update on the Facilities Committee and expressed appreciation for the district’s Ramadan meals-to-go program.

Ms. Creason acknowledged the importance of booster organizations and encouraged outreach to schools with lower parent
participation. Ms. Creason also reported that she visited the ABLED organization, which offers a job-training program for
adults with intellectual and developmental disabilities and serves former students who have aged out of district programs.
Additionally, Ms. Creason provided an update on the American Indian Education Program Parent Advisory Committee
(AIEP PAC), where she serves as the committee liaison.

Future Agenda (L)
An item was added to explore ways to develop an educational program addressing the effects of social media, which Ms.
Creason suggested and a majority of board members supported.

Adjournment (N)
At 7:58 p.m., there being no further business, the regular meeting was adjourned.

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                      AGENDA ITEM:                      C.1
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                      MEETING DATE:                 03/24/2026

SUBJECT: School Library Month (Dale)

DEPARTMENT: Teaching and Learning

ACTION REQUESTED:
Action: The superintendent is recommending that the board adopt Resolution No. A-463, recognizing the month of
April as School Library Month.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The resolution recognizes the month of April as School Library Month, in partnership with the American
Association of School Librarians, and acknowledges continued district support for the school libraries and the staff
who support them, including our teacher librarians. School libraries and teacher librarians make a positive impact
on students. Research across more than 25 states shows a relationship between a quality school library and student
achievement. Additionally, our school libraries are vibrant places of learning where all students can access critical
learning resources, such as reliable Internet and rich reading material. San Juan Unified believes that a well-
rounded education includes access to school libraries which fosters critical thinking, self-expression, lifelong
learning skills, and readiness for college and career.

ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Resolution No. A-463

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
N/A

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 03/16/2026

FISCAL IMPACT:
N/A

PREPARED BY:                 Nicole Naditz, Program Specialist, Instructional Technology and World Languages
                             Nicole Kukral, Director, Professional Learning and Curriculum Innovation

APPROVED BY:                 Timothy C. Dale, Ed.D., Deputy Superintendent, Schools and Student Support
                             Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools




                                                                                                                        9
                                    SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT
                                            Resolution No. A-463

                                                  School Library Month
                                                       April, 2026

WHEREAS, we strongly believe that teacher librarians are an essential part of a well-rounded education from preschool
through grade 12; and

WHEREAS, through their expertise in information literacy, digital citizenship, and fostering a love of reading, teacher
librarians help students develop critical thinking, problem-solving, communication, collaboration, self-expression, and
lifelong learning skills; and

WHEREAS, classified school library staff play a vital role in supporting elementary and middle school students by
creating welcoming spaces that foster a love of reading, with their hard work keeping school libraries running smoothly,
providing essential support to students and staff;

WHEREAS, the Every Student Succeeds Act (ESSA) fully supports a “well-rounded education” with the goal of fully
preparing all students for success in college and careers; and

WHEREAS, research has demonstrated that school libraries and teacher librarians contribute to higher academic
achievement, improved reading scores, and increased student engagement; and

WHEREAS, thousands of students in San Juan Unified benefit from the resources and support provided by our
dedicated teacher librarians in the classroom and in the library; and

NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED that the Board of Education proposes continued support of teacher
librarians and classified library staff. This resolution gives recognition to the students in our district and our dedicated
teacher librarians and classified library staff.

BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED that the Board of Education applauds the demonstrated enthusiasm and dedication of
the San Juan Unified teacher librarians and classified library staff who inspire a love of reading, support academic
achievement, and foster a culture of inquiry and learning in our schools and in our community.

Attested to this 24th day of March 2026                     ________________________________

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                    AGENDA ITEM:                     C.2
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                    MEETING DATE:                03/24/2026

SUBJECT: Week of the Young Child (Townsend-Snider)

DEPARTMENT: Teaching and Learning

ACTION REQUESTED:
Action: The superintendent is recommending that the board adopt Resolution No. A-464, proclaiming the week of
April 11-17, 2026, as the Week of the Young Child.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The Week of the Young Child is a nationally recognized annual celebration held each April to highlight the
importance of high-quality early childhood education and to recognize the educators and families who support
young children during their most critical years of development. April 11–17, 2026, is being recognized nationwide
as the Week of the Young Child.

San Juan Unified School District is committed to supporting learning from birth through continuing throughout
life. The District’s Early Childhood Education Department serves thousands of families through infant/toddler,
preschool, and before and after-school programs, providing educational, social, emotional, and health-related
support. In partnership with other district departments and community programs, the department plays a vital role
in fostering school readiness and family engagement.

Recognition of this week affirms the District’s ongoing commitment to investing in and supporting its youngest
learners.

ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Resolution No. A-464

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
N/A

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 03/16/2026

FISCAL IMPACT:
N/A

PREPARED BY:                Rebecca Marsolais, Director II, Early Childhood Education and Extended Learning

APPROVED BY:                Amberlee Townsend-Snider, Asst. Superintendent, Elementary Education & Programs
                            Timothy C. Dale, Ed.D., Deputy Superintendent, Schools and Student Support
                            Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools




                                                                                                                    11
                                        SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT
                                               Resolution No. A-464

                                              WEEK OF THE YOUNG CHILD
                                                   April 11-17, 2026


WHEREAS, the San Juan Unified School District has a commitment to encourage and support learning
beginning at birth and continuing throughout a person's life; and

WHEREAS, April 11-17, 2026, is being recognized as Week of the Young Child throughout the United
States; and

WHEREAS, the Early Childhood Education Department has primary responsibility for providing quality
educational, social, and emotional services to meet the diverse needs of thousands of families and children
in the infant/toddler, preschool and before and after school programs; and

WHEREAS, thousands of parents and caregivers receive their first family education and family involvement
experiences through the Early Childhood Education Department; and

WHEREAS, hundreds of children receive mental and physical health follow-up through the Early Childhood
Education Department; and

WHEREAS, other departments and programs such as Birth and Beyond, Adult Education, Special
Education, Student Assistance and Prevention Programs and P-6 teachers, counselors, nurses and
administrators serve as partners with the Early Childhood Education Department;

NOW THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED by the San Juan Unified School District Board of Education, that it
recognizes the week of April 11-17, 2026, as Week of the Young Child in honor of the outstanding services
provided by Early Childhood Education staff members in the San Juan Unified School District; and

BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED that the district commends San Juan Unified’s Early Childhood Education
administrators, teachers and other staff members for their significant contributions to the education of
infants, toddlers, preschoolers, and young children in our community.

Attested to this 24th day of March 2026                  ________________________________

BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                     MEETING DATE:                03/24/2026

SUBJECT: New High School Courses for 2026-2027

DEPARTMENT: Teaching and Learning

ACTION REQUESTED:
The superintendent is recommending that the board approve the new proposed high school courses. (Discussed:
03/10/2026).

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The proposed high school courses of study listed below are designed to ensure student choice and instructional
alignment with the Strategic Plan, Local Control and Accountability Plan (LCAP), and California’s State
Standards. The proposed courses of study provide a high level of rigor, deeper opportunities for study, and promote
college and career readiness. Additionally, the Personal Finance course addresses the new California high school
graduation requirement of Personal Finance, effective for the graduating class of 2030-2031. New high school
courses are available for review in the superintendent’s office.

Courses:
     Arts:
             Commercial Music/Modern Band
             Jazz Choir
             Mariachi Ensemble
             Musical Theater
             Percussion Ensemble
      Electives:
             Personal Finance*
             Philosophy
             Project Management

*Personal Finance is a new California graduation requirement, effective for the class of 2030-31, and must be
offered by 2027-28.

ATTACHMENT(S):
1. 2026-2027 New Course Descriptions

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
Curriculum, Standards, Instructional & Student Services Committee: 02/11/2026 (discussion); 03/04/2026 (action)
PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 03/02/2026, 03/16/2026
Board of Education: 03/10/2026 (discussion)

FISCAL IMPACT:
N/A

                            Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools




                                                                                                                      30
                                                                                                    Attachment 1
                                San Juan Unified School District
                             New High School Courses for 2026-2027
                                     Rationale/Background




Commercial Music/Modern Band (A-G):
Commercial Music/Modern Band introduces students to the performance and production of popular music
genres including rock, pop, R&B, and hip-hop. Students learn guitar, bass, drums, keyboard, and vocals, as
well as songwriting, recording, and music technology. Modern Band broadens access to music education
by centering student interest and cultural relevance. It connects performance with creativity and technology,
fostering engagement and musical literacy through contemporary styles.

Jazz Choir (A-G):
Jazz Choir is a performance-based course focused on the study and performance of jazz repertoire, including
swing, Latin, blues, and contemporary vocal jazz. Students develop ensemble singing techniques, jazz
phrasing, and improvisation skills while exploring the cultural roots of jazz music. Public performances are
an integral part of the course. This course expands the choral program by introducing students to jazz
performance practice, ensemble collaboration, and improvisational artistry. It fosters creativity, teamwork,
and an appreciation for America’s rich jazz heritage.

Mariachi Ensemble (A-G):
Mariachi Ensemble is a year-long, performance-based course that explores the vibrant musical traditions of
mariachi as a reflection of Mexican and Mexican American cultural identity, resistance, and celebration.
Students learn to perform authentic mariachi repertoire on traditional instruments (violin, trumpet,
guitarrón, vihuela, and guitar) while studying the historical, social, and cultural contexts that shaped this art
form. Through rehearsals, performances, and reflective discussion, students connect music to broader
themes of identity and community. Mariachi is one of the most visible and celebrated musical traditions in
the Americas—rooted in Indigenous, African, and European influences and reflecting centuries of cultural
exchange and resilience.

Musical Theater (A-G):
Musical Theater is a performance-based course combining acting, singing, and movement. Students explore
musical theater repertoire from classic to contemporary productions, learning stagecraft, vocal technique,
and character development through rehearsal and performance. This course develops students’ artistic
expression through integrated performance arts. It fosters confidence, teamwork, and communication skills
while offering authentic experience in the creation and presentation of musical theater productions.

Percussion Ensemble (A-G):
Percussion Ensemble focuses on the study and performance of percussion literature spanning classical,
world, and contemporary styles. Students develop technical skills on a variety of instruments, including
mallets, drums, and auxiliary percussion. The course emphasizes rhythmic accuracy, ensemble cohesion,
and stylistic interpretation. This course aligns with the band program, providing percussionists with a
focused environment to build musicianship, technique, and ensemble skills. It supports both concert and
marching band participation and promotes collaboration within the larger music department.

Personal Finance (A-G):
Personal Finance is a semester-long course designed to deliver essential financial understanding in an
engaging way. By completing this course, students will have the foundational knowledge to properly plan
their financial futures post-graduation and make sound financial decisions in their present and adult lives.
Students will learn personal finance through comprehensive lessons, activities, and projects using current
media and real-world scenarios. Personal Finance expands upon a student’s finance, math, reading, and
critical thinking skills. The following topics will be covered: personal banking, budgeting, employment,
credit, loans, insurance, taxes, investing, consumer protection, financing college and career training,
behavioral economics, and charitable giving.

                                                                                                                    31
                                                                                                  Attachment 1



Philosophy (A-G):
This is an introductory course that covers all the major fields of philosophy, including theory of knowledge,
epistemology, philosophy of mind, ethics, logic, social and political philosophy, and philosophy of religion.
Students will study the great philosophers through their ideas and their rhetoric. They will study how the
philosophers use language and rhetoric to not only give shape to their ideas, but also to coherently
communicate themselves. Through original writing, students will practice logical, argumentative writing to
justify their own philosophical perspectives. Finally, students will synthesize ideas of the great philosophers
to make sense of contemporary ideas and issues in American society.

Project Management (A-G):
In this course students learn not only how to manage a process, but how to lead people. As students learn
about the process of project management, they will develop skills in managing deliverables, deadlines,
schedules, and scope. As they learn to lead people (team members, customers, and clients), students will
develop skills in how to motivate and empower others to implement effective project management
techniques. Acquiring these processes and people skills will ensure students are not only prepared to
perform within a project based academic environment but will also be able to apply their understanding of
project management to their entrepreneurial ventures.

SUBJECT: Head Start (HS) and Early Head Start (EHS) Grant Application 2026-2027

DEPARTMENT: Teaching and Learning

ACTION REQUESTED:
The superintendent is recommending that the board approve the submission and implementation of, if funded, the
Head Start and Early Head Start Grant 2026-2027.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The annual Head Start and Early Head Start grant application seeks to renew funding for the 2026-2027 fiscal year.
These programs provide comprehensive services for pregnant women and their children from infancy to age five,
supporting education, health, mental health, career development, adult education, and access to community
resources. These programs play a crucial role in supporting early childhood development and ensure children are
prepared for academic success at young ages.

​3. ​Locally Designed Option (LDO).​​If the agency continues to operate an LDO option
     ​and requests to add a​​new​​center or class that will​​require a class-size waiver. This
      ​waiver would be used under three circumstances:
          o ​To accommodate a transitioning EHS child into a Head Start classroom in the
          ​
             ​same center. In these cases, the waiver is used when an EHS child is aging
              ​out at 36 months and is eligible for Head Start services on the same campus,
               ​but the class is capped at 17. Documentation will be maintained in each
                ​child’s file when this option is exercised.
          o ​To accommodate a transitioning EHS child into a Head Start classroom in an
          ​
                 ​adjacent location. In these cases, the waiver is used when an EHS child is
                  ​aging out at 36 months and is eligible for Head Start services at the nearest
                   ​location, but the class is capped at 17. Documentation will be maintained in
                    ​each child’s file when this option is exercised.
          o ​To accommodate a child with disabilities in a Head Start full-inclusion
          ​
                     ​classroom that has been capped at 17.

​4.​ ​Other comments, please include changes in program leadership, organizational​
      ​structure, and program service delivery.​
     ​We are shifting capacity between the Howe Ave and Encina site to better align with​
    ​actual demand. The following changes will be implemented:​
          ​Howe Rm J2-​​The enrollment cap will be lowered from​​24 to 16 slots. This​
           ​reduction reflects a decline in the waiting list at the Howe Ave site. Howe does​
            ​have three classrooms, providing more overall space despite the localized​
             ​decline in waiting list numbers.​
          ​Encina U4-​​The enrollment​​cap will be increased from 16 to 24 slots to​
           ​accommodate the growing waiting list at this location. Currently, Encina​
            ​operates with one classroom but is experiencing a higher demand, as​
             ​evidenced by the increasing waiting list.​

                          San Juan Uniﬁed School District
                             Early Childhood Education
                         Early Head Start Budget Narrative
                      Fiscal Year August 1, 2026 – July 31, 2027
                             TOTAL AWARD: $2,937,877
The San Juan Unified School District Early Head Start Grant will be used as follows:
   ● Basic Funds: $2,906,965
   ● Training and Technical Assistance (TTA): $30,912


Personnel (Schedule A): $1,652,275
      Personnel costs include salaries for staff directly related to Early Head Start
      (EHS) classroom instruction and related activities for the following:
         ● Salaries for Teachers working in the Home-Based portion of the EHS
             Program
         ● Salaries for Teachers and Non-Instructional Support Aides in the
             year-round Center-Based program
         ● Salaries for Teachers and Non-Instructional Support Aides in the hybrid
             10-month Center-Based and Home Visit model that are also year-round.

      Personnel costs also include salaries for Content Specialists and Teachers on
      Special Assignment (TOSAs) to ensure that Early Head Start services are
      planned, implemented, and monitored. Content Specialists and TOSAs provide
      coaching support for classroom Teachers and Non-Instructional Support Aides.
      Each content specialist possesses expertise in one or more of the following
      content areas:
         ● Disability Services
         ● Education
         ● Nutrition
         ● Eligibility, Recruitment, Selection, Enrollment, and Attendance (ERSEA)
         ● Parent Community and Family Engagement
         ● Safe Environments

      Mandates for Program Design and Management are met with partial salaries of
      the following:
         ● Early Education Program Director (1)
         ● Program Administrators (2)
         ● ECE Support Specialist (1)
         ● ECE Classified Supervisor (1)
         ● Program Analyst (1)



                                                                                        1
                                                                                            54
          ● Fiscal Technician I (3)
          ● Program & Admin Secretaries (3)

       A percentage of the Early Education Program Director and assigned Secretary
       salaries are paid through the Early Head Start Grant to ensure that
       communication between the EHS Program, other ECE, and San Juan programs,
       including the Governing Board, continues to occur regularly. The ECE Program
       Director also provides a vital link to services related to Indirect Costs, such as
       Legal Services, Payroll, Maintenance and Operations, Purchasing, Fiscal Services,
       and Budget Services.

       Other staff salaries related to providing services to support the program that will
       also be funded from the Head Start grant include:
           •     School Community Workers who assist in the Family Partnership
                 process, as well as support the provision of community resources and
                 parent education.
           •     Admissions and Family Service Technicians who facilitate registration
                 for the Head Start Program
           •     Additional Secretaries who provide support needed for (ERSEA)
                 services and monitoring in the registration process.
           •     Nurses, Health Assistants, and an Intermediate Clerk Typist to support
                 health team functions.
           •     Sub-Desk Secretary and Intermediate Clerk Typist to support
                 classrooms by maintaining and filling substitute needs and managing
                 front office reception services for parents.

Fringe Benefits (Schedule B): $823,373
      All staff contracted to work for San Juan are subject to Fringe Benefits. These
      benefits include Medical/Dental/Vision plans, retirement, life insurance, and
      disability plans. Payroll-related costs of 24.99% will be applied to all certificated
      employees, and 40.84% will be applied to all classified staff. In addition, the
      average Early Childhood Education department Health and Welfare cost of
      $14,720 applies to all permanent staff contracted for four or more hours per day.

Supplies (Schedule E): $84,304
  1.      Office Supplies: purchase of supplies for Early Head Start office needs. The
          supplies are 100% administrative in nature.
  2.      Child and Family Services Supplies: Consumables that are needed for
          classroom-related activities.




                                                                                              2
                                                                                                  55
   3.     Food Services Supplies: Early Head Start classrooms are not vended for
          snacks. However, snacks will be provided for children who are hungry
          between meals. Purchases may be made through established purchase orders
          at Smart & Final and Bel-Air/Raley’s. These funds help promote healthy food
          choices for students and families.
   4.     Other Supplies: Items which exceed $499.00 in cost — including any
          applicable taxes — that are not considered consumables. These items include
          furniture and classroom technological needs.

Other (Schedule H): $180,400
   1.    Utilities/Telephone: Wireless connection is required for EHS Home-Based
         Teachers to conduct required program business when providing in-home
         services to children and families enrolled in the Home-Based Program.
   2.    Building and Child Liability Insurance: Child Liability Insurance is purchased
         annually for Early Head Start children to meet the required compliance.
   3.    Copy Machine Maintenance: annual maintenance agreements that support
         classroom and administrative printing needs to provide adequate program
         services.
   4.    Local Travel: Mileage reimbursement for staff making Home Visits or
         conducting program business. Rate of reimbursement is determined by the
         IRS mileage rate at the time driving occurred.
   5.    Nutrition Services: Vended food costs for children that exceed the
         reimbursement from the Child Care Food Program are charged back to the
         Early Head Start budget.
   6.    Substitutes: Substitute staffing is needed to replace personnel when absent to
         ensure that the program maintains the required adult/student ratios and
         mandatory daily tasks are completed. All substitutes are on an on-call basis
         and do not receive fringe benefits such as Health & Welfare and Life
         Insurance.
   7.    Parent Services: Costs for teacher or administrator meetings with families for
         students enrolled in the Early Head Start program.
   8.    Publications/Advertising/Printing: Printing costs consist of Parent and Staff
         Handbooks, resource materials, enrollment forms, and classroom
         instructional materials.
   9.    Other: Costs include organizational memberships that include Region IX and
         Head Start California, and Site Licensing Fees paid to the California
         Department of Social Services, and the cost of contracts or service agreements
         used for training. Also included in these line items are expenses towards any
         medical supplies required, as the program is a Payor of Last Resort in specific
         instances.



                                                                                       3
                                                                                           56
Indirect Charges: $166,613
      Indirect charges on all expenses except capitalized and non-capitalized assets
      will be charged at a rate of 6.03%, which is calculated by the California
      Standardized Account Code System. The approved Indirect Cost Rate
      Agreement is established between the State of California Department of
      Education (CDE), and San Juan Unified. An annual wage compensation report is
      prepared, and the district allowed indirect charge rate is adjusted by CDE
      accordingly.

Training and Technical Assistance (Schedule H): $30,912
      Training and Technical Assistance funds will be used for the following:
      1.     Training and Staff Development costs include:
                • Teaching Pyramid to increase social-emotional curriculum &
                    support for teachers in managing student classroom behaviors.
                • Leadership Team Professional Development and training for Early
                    Head Start staff as pre-service prior to the start of the academic year
                    and continuing throughout the year to improve collaboration and
                    communication skills, as well as reviewing grant and program
                    requirements.
                • Family Engagement and Training Workshops to help familiarize
                    families with SJUSD’s Head Start Program and encourage family
                    involvement in the program and student success.
                • Annual CPR training and recertification for staff as needed to
                    ensure program compliance with licensing requirements. Both new
                    and continuing staff who attend will be CPR certified.
                • Conferences for staff and parents include the California Head Start
                    Conferences on Family Engagement, Research, Education, and
                    Health and Management, NAEYC.

Cost Allocation Methodology
      Certified in accordance with 2 CFR 200.416, San Juan Unified has an Indirect Rate
      reviewed and approved by the cognizant agency, the California Department of
      Education. Any additional program costs not included in the Indirect Rate
      proposal are allocated as follows:
                 • San Juan’s Early Head Start program collaborates with the
                     state-funded General Child Care grant (CCTR) and San Juan’s
                     Local Control Funding Formula (LCFF) Supplemental Funds to
                     share resources. Teachers are either partially paid or fully
                     supported through these funds. These costs are allocated based on
                     the number of children being served in the collaborative



                                                                                         4
                                                                                              57
                  partnership, program operational hours, and program
                  requirements for each class.
                • Additional program costs (proportionately salary & benefits)
                  benefiting multiple funding streams apart from the CCTR, LCFF,
                  and Early Heart Start grant are allocated based upon the approved
                  per student allocation methodology, and any exceptions are based
                  upon monthly submissions of Personal Activity Reports.

Non-Federal Share: $734,470
     The required 25% of Non-Federal matching funds of $734,470 is reflected in the
     current budget for Early Head Start and is 25% of the total basic budget. The
     non-federal match is comprised of in-kind personnel:
        1.     Personnel
               • CCTR Teacher Salaries                                    $     95,626
               • CCTR Non-Instructional Support Aides                     $     73,238
               • LCFF Teacher Salaries                                    $    130,240
               • Other Administrative/Support Staff                       $    137,532
                                                       Sub-Total:​        $    436,636

          2.    Benefits                                                 $    297,834

                                        Total Personnel & Benefits:​     $    734,470

                                                     Total In-Kind:​     $    734,470

      Other Funding Resources
      •     Federally Funded: Child and Adult Care Food Program          $     158,413
      •     Title I Funds                                                $      12,000
      •     LCFF Supplemental Funds                                      $     550,000
      •     General Child Care and Development (CCTR) Grant              $   1,920,237

                                                            Total:       $   2,640,650

Financial Management System
      San Juan uses Frontline Escape for its accounting and reporting system. All
      funds, federal, state, local, or parent-generated, are maintained within the Cash
      in County Treasury with the Sacramento County Office of Education, and
      financial reconciliation procedures are completed monthly. San Juan’s Budget
      and Fiscal Services Department ensures effective oversight and accountability for
      federal funds by conducting ongoing internal reviews. San Juan’s accounting



                                                                                         5
                                                                                             58
system allows for individual accounts and multiple levels of resource centers,
allowing expenditure reports by various funding sources to be collected,
reviewed, and audited for reasonable and allowable costs. It is from these
reports that monthly fiscal reports are submitted to the grantee, Sacramento
Employment Training Agency, for reimbursement of expenses, and to San Juan’s
Governing Board, Policy Committee Board, and Program Management for
review and approval of expenditures, budget development, and program
planning.

                            Head Start Budget Narrative
                      Fiscal Year August 1, 2026 – July 31, 2027
                             TOTAL AWARD: $7,690,319

The San Juan Unified Head Start Grant will be used as follows:
   ● Basic Funds: $7,656,919
   ● Training & Technical Assistance (TTA) Funds: $33,400

Personnel (Schedule A): $4,056,684
      Personnel costs include salaries for staff directly related to Head Start (HS)
      classroom instruction and related activities for the following:
          ● Classroom Teachers
          ● Child Development Assistants
          ● Non-Instructional Support Aides
          ● Bi-Lingual Instructional Assistants

       Personnel costs also include salaries for Content Specialists and Teachers on
       Special Assignment (TOSAs) to ensure that Head Start services are planned,
       implemented, and monitored. Content Specialists and TOSAs provide coaching
       support for classroom Teachers, Child Development Assistants, and
       Non-Instructional Support Aides. Each content specialist possesses expertise in
       one or more of the following content areas:
          ● Disability Services
          ● Nutrition
          ● Education
          ● Eligibility, Recruitment, Selection, Enrollment, and Attendance (ERSEA)
          ● Parent Community and Family Engagement
          ● Safe Environments

       Mandates for Program Design and Management are met with partial salaries of
       the following:
          •      Early Education Program Director (1)
          •      Program Administrators (2)
          •      ECE Support Specialist (1)
          •      ECE Classified Supervisor (1)
          •      Program Analyst (1)
          •      Fiscal Technician I (3)
          •      Program & Admin Secretaries (3)

Other (Schedule H): $518,500
   1.​   Building and Child Liability Insurance: A Student Insurance policy is

   2.​   Copy Machine Maintenance: annual maintenance agreements that support

         conducting program business. Rate of reimbursement is determined by the

   4.​   Nutrition Services: vended food costs for children that exceed the

   5.​   Substitutes: Substitute staffing is needed to replace personnel when absent to

   7.​   Publications/Advertising/Printing: Printing costs consist of Parent and Staff
         Handbooks, resource materials, enrollment forms, and classroom
         instructional materials.
   8.​   Training or Staff Development: costs for on-site and/or online trainings for
         program staff not covered under the TTA plan. These costs also include
         employee compensation for time attending training or staff development
         outside of contracted hours.
   9.​   Local Conferences: for staff and eligible parents to support high-quality
         training in areas that are program-applicable and not covered under TTA
         spending plan.

         Licensing Fees paid to the California Department of Social Services, and the

         line item are expenses towards any medical supplies required, as the program

      Indirect charges on all expenses except capitalized and non-capitalized assets

Training and Technical Assistance (TTA): $33,400
      Training and Technical Assistance funds will be used for the following (see
      Schedule H):
      1.​    Training and Staff Development costs include:
                •​ Conferences for both staff and parents include the Head Start
                    California, Region IX, HS Association Conferences on Family
                    Engagement, Research, Education, and Health and Management.
                •​ Leadership Team Professional Development and training for Head
                    Start staff as pre-service prior to the start of the academic year and
                    continuing throughout the year to improve collaboration and
                    communication skills, as well as reviewing grant and program
                    requirements.
                •​ Desired Results Developmental Profile (DRDP) training for staff
                    that covers updates and best practices for data collection and
                    analysis
                •​ Teaching Pyramid and Wellness Symposium to increase student
                    social-emotional support for teachers in managing classroom
                    behaviors and supporting educator wellness and sustainable work
                    practices.
                •​ Family Engagement and Training Workshops to help familiarize
                    families with SJUSD’s Head Start Program and encourage family
                    involvement in the program and student success.
                •​ Annual CPR training and recertification for staff as needed to
                    ensure program compliance with licensing requirements. Both new
                    and continuing staff who attend will be CPR certified.
                •​ CLASS Training for educational staff that focuses on training to
                    improve teacher-child interactions and for completing required
                    observations and evaluations for program improvement.

                       TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE PLAN
                                                                    EARLY HEAD START
                                                                        2026 - 2027

PHILOSOPHY
San Juan Unified School District’s Early Head Start program’s philosophy is to provide quality training for staff in order to support success for children and
families. ​

PLANNING PROCESS
The planning process for the Training and Technical Assistance Plan incorporated information gathered from self-assessment, staff surveys, Grantee Monitoring,
current Program Improvement Plans, Child Outcomes, and established county-wide goals. Parent Policy Committee members review, make recommendations, and
approve the plan.

APPROACH TO TRAINING, TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE, AND PROGRAM IMPROVEMENT
San Juan Unified School District Early Head Start will be utilizing the results of the self-assessment to improve organizational structures, provide essential training
and support for all staff, increase family engagement, and ensure high-quality care for our students by serving their school readiness, nutritional, and
social-emotional needs. Data from the self-assessment, Program Improvement Plan, 5-year goals updates, and performance standards are all incorporated into the
Training and Technical Assistance plan. School readiness data, staff, and parent input are included in the data that is analyzed.

TRAINING DELIVERY
SJUSD Early Head Start offers a variety of formal and informal forums to ensure professional growth and staff development are current with all staff and program
needs and requirements. Training is provided utilizing the following methods:
   ● Pre-service training at the beginning of the year provides a look at new initiatives and procedures, as well as a review of program focal areas.
   ● Monthly—One full-day Thursday Professional Development is offered once a month, 7 times per year. Topics will relate to five-year goals, Health,
        Self-assessment, School Readiness, Classroom Assessment Scoring System (CLASS), ITERS, Creative Curriculum, Grant and Licensing requirements,
        and staff input relating to needed staff development topics.
   ● Individualized Coaching Model is provided by Content Specialists, TOSAs, and internally trained presenters.
   ● New Teacher Training—Additional training is provided to new teachers. Topics include assessments, planning, individualizing, curriculum, parent
        involvement, nutrition, health and safety, mental health, and children with disabilities.
   ● Training Events—Select training topics may be offered on evenings and weekends, utilizing community partners and local agencies.
   ● ECE Staff will attend the California Head Start Association Conferences to remain up to date on best practices, as well as maintain connections to
        Regional and State/ Head Start Agencies.




                                                                                                                                                             1
                                                                                                                                                                          75
TRAINING, MONITORING AND EVALUATION
All training provided includes an opportunity for staff input through a Training Evaluation Form. Evaluation forms are collected and reviewed to ensure the
content of the training meets the needs of the participants. Surveys are analyzed by the program management and leadership team to assess satisfaction through a
continuous improvement process.

                                               TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE PLAN
                                                             HEAD START
                                                              2026-2027

PHILOSOPHY
San Juan Unified School District’s Head Start program’s philosophy is to provide quality training for staff in order to support success for children and families.

PLANNING PROCESS
The planning process for the Training and Technical Assistance Plan incorporates information gathered from self-assessment, staff surveys, Grantee Monitoring,
current Program Improvement Plan, Child Outcomes, and established county-wide goals. Parent Policy Committee members review, make recommendations, and
approve the plan.

APPROACH TO TRAINING, TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE, AND PROGRAM IMPROVEMENT
San Juan Unified School District Head Start will be utilizing the results of the self-assessment to improve organizational structures, provide essential training and
support for all staff, increase family engagement, and ensure high-quality care for our students by serving their school readiness, nutritional, and social-emotional
needs. Data from the self-assessment, Program Improvement Plan, 5-year goals updates, and performance standards are all incorporated into the Training and
Technical Assistance plan. School readiness data, staff, and parent input are included in the data that is analyzed.

TRAINING DELIVERY
San Juan Unified School District Head Start offers a variety of formal and informal forums to ensure professional growth and staff development are current with
staff and program needs and requirements. Training is provided utilizing the following methods:
     ● Pre-service training at the beginning of the year provides a look at new initiatives and procedures, as well as a review of program focal areas.
     ● Monthly—Three-hour training on specific training topics each month. Sessions are offered on the 1st and 3rd Thursday of every month from 1:00-4:00 pm
         during the school year. Topics will relate to the five-year goal, Self-assessment findings, School Readiness goals, CLASS, Health, and other topics that
         may come up during the school year.
     ● Individualized Coaching Model is provided by Content Specialists, TOSAs, and internally trained presenters.
     ● New Teacher Training—Additional training is provided to new teachers. Topics include assessments, planning, individualizing, curriculum, parent
         involvement, nutrition, health and safety, mental health, and children with disabilities.
     ● Training Events—Select training topics may be offered on evenings and weekends, utilizing community partners and local agencies.
     ● ECE Staff will attend the California Head Start Association Conferences to remain up to date on best practices, as well as maintain connections to
         Regional and State/ Head Start Agencies.

All training provided includes an opportunity for staff input through a Training Evaluation Form. Evaluation forms are collected and reviewed to ensure the

                                                    Goal #1 Increase child outcomes by developing and strengthening social emo onal competencies, family partnerships, and staﬀ development.

                                                OBJECTIVE 1 Establish a shared defini on, language, and understanding across staﬀ and parents in regard to social emo onal competencies (SECs).
                                                             Is this a new Objec ve for Year 3 ? YES___NO_x_ If yes, please explain the ra onale for changing or adjus ng your Objec ve. Tip: Refer to the
                                                             Year 1 Closeout chart describing challenges and how they informed your decision.
                                                             1. Social Emo onal Competencies informa on will be provided at all parent conferences.
                                                             2. Three parent workshops will be given each year for new incoming parents to learn about SECs.
Measurable Outcomes (Upon compleƟon of ObjecƟve)
                                                             3. Informa onal document on SECs will be provided to every family during the program orienta on.
                                                             4. Informa onal document on SECs will be provided to all new staﬀ during on-boarding process.
                                                             1. Targeted small group trainings that will expand on SEC development in the classroom environment.
                                                             2. Developing addi onal topics for parent workshops, including ayschronous op ons.
Year 3 Expected Outcomes
                                                             3. Increase survey ques ons around SEC development when famlies are providing feedback
                                                             4. Quarterly review of SECs during New Teacher Training mee ngs.

                                                             2. Dra s of Documents
Data, Tools, or Methods for Tracking Progress
                                                             3. Sign-in sheets and Agendas from the Policy Commi ee (PC) mee ng
                                                             4. Sign-in Sheet and Agendas from staﬀ training
                                                             1. Iden fying topics that will provide staﬀ with a variety of op ons during breakout sessions.
                                                             2. Increasing family a endence at workshops and Parent cafes
Expected Challenges/How you might address them
                                                             3. Providing surveys that meet the lanaguage needs of each family.
                                                             4. Providing me to monitor staﬀ understanding of SEC development.

                         OBJECTIVE 1

                                                OBJECTIVE 2 Build mastery across all classroom staﬀ around social emo onal competencies (SECs) and rela onship building.
                                                             Is this a new Objec ve for Year 3? YES___NO_x__ If yes, please explain the ra onale for changing or adjus ng your Objec ve. Tip: Refer to the

                                                             1. All classroom teachers iden fy social-emo onal competencies on their lesson plans.

Measurable Outcomes (Upon compleƟon of ObjecƟve)
                                                             3. Established digital resource library that is updated regularly and u lized by all classroom staﬀ.
                                                             4. Consistent training on social-emo onal competency is oﬀered quarterly.
                                                             1. 70% of classroom teachers iden fy social-emo onal competencies on their lesson plans.

Year 3 Expected Outcomes
                                                             3. Digital Resource Library is linked for staﬀ monthly using the newsle er system, with updates and feedback op ons provided.

GOALS & OBJECTIVES
                                                            Year 3 of 5
                                                            1. Lesson plans data is collected twice per year by the content specialist.
                                                            2. Social Emo onal Development scores on DRDP will increase.
Data, Tools, or Methods for Tracking Progress
                                                            3. CLASS scores in the posi ve climate domain will increase.
                                                            4. Leadership team roster, agendas, and mee ng minutes.
                                                            1. Accurate monitoring of lesson plans, both by digital submission and site visits across classrooms and support staﬀ. Crea ng a shared rubric
                                                            for support staﬀ to provide monitoring during site visits.
                                                            2. Providing training to all staﬀ on implemen ng Second Step in a manner that meets the learning needs of each team. U lize a variety of
                                                            examples and strategies (video, modeling, group turn-and-talks, etc.) to increase consistency.
Expected Challenges/How you might address them
                                                            3. Iden fying digital resources that are current, developmentally appropriate, and target the needs of students and families. Reach out to staﬀ
                                                            and families for feedback on resources they find useful for their classrooms and homes.
                                                            4. Accessing a convenient loca on for all staﬀ and iden fying me for coaching, training, and monitoring to take place. Collaborate with
                                                            administra on and secretarial staﬀ to streamline a standing date and loca on for mee ngs.

GOALS & OBJECTIVES
                                                                         Year 3 of 5
 AGENCY NAME
 San Juan Unified School District
 DATE
 8/1/2026-7/31/2027
                                                                         Increase the health, safety and wellbeing of children birth to age 5 by expanding communica on systems and educa on that will include
                                                               Goal #2

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                      AGENDA ITEM:                     F.9
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                      MEETING DATE:                 03/24/2026

SUBJECT: James Cowan Fundamental Elementary School Site Security Project CEQA Notice of Exemption and
Categorical Exemption

DEPARTMENT: Operations

ACTION REQUESTED:
The superintendent is recommending that the board approve the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA)
Notice of Exemption and Categorical Exemption for the James Cowan Fundamental Elementary School site
security project.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The proposed project is located entirely within James Cowan Fundamental Elementary School’s campus at 3350
Becerra Way (Assessor Parcel Numbers: 255-0194-016-0000, 255-0194-027-0000, and 255-0331-025-0000), in
Sacramento, Sacramento County, California. The project area spans approximately 0.89 of an acre of primarily
developed and landscaped areas within the school’s boundaries. The project scope includes the construction of site
fencing and gates adjacent to the school frontage, including an accessible egress gate, accessible pedestrian gates,
and maintenance gates.

As the actions associated with the proposed project are not expected to result in a significant environmental
impact, it is expected that this project is exempt under the Class III Categorical (New Construction or Conversion
of Small Structures) and Class XIV Categorical Exemption (Minor Additions to Schools), as described in the
California Code of Regulations, Title 15, Section 15300 et seq.

ATTACHMENT(S):
1. James Cowan Fundamental Elementary School Site Security Project CEQA Notice of Exemption and Categorical
Exemption Memorandum Terracon Project No: NB267030

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
N/A

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 03/16/2026

FISCAL IMPACT:
N/A
PREPARED BY:                 Nicholas Arps, Director, Facilities, Construction & Modernization
APPROVED BY:                 Frank Camarda, Chief Operations Officer
                             Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools




                                                                                                                       116
                                                Attachment 1




Memorandum

TO:             Nicholas Arps
                P: (916) 971-5780
                E: Nicholas.arps@sanjuan.edu


                San Juan Unified School District
                3738 Walnut Avenue
                Carmichael, California 95608


FROM:           Terracon Consultants, Inc.

DATE:           March 10, 2026

RE:             James Cowan Fundamental Elementary School Site Security Project
                CEQA Notice of Exemption and Categorical Exemption Memorandum
                Terracon Project No: NB267030



Project Information:
The proposed project is located entirely within James Cowan Fundamental Elementary
at 3350 Becerra Way (Assessor Parcel Numbers [APNs]: 255-0194-016-0000, 255-0194-027-0000, and
255-0331-025-0000), in Sacramento, Sacramento County, California. The project area spans
approximately 0.89 of an acre of primarily developed and landscaped areas within
boundaries (Exhibit 1). The project scope includes the construction of site fencing and gates adjacent
to the school frontage, including an accessible egress gate, accessible pedestrian gates, and
maintenance gates.

As the actions associated with the proposed project are not expected to result in a significant
environmental impact, it is expected that this project is exempt under the Class III Categorical (New
Construction or Conversion of Small Structures) and Class XIV Categorical Exemption (Minor Additions
to Schools), as described in the California Code of Regulations, Title 15, Section 15300 et seq.


Legal Setting:
Categorical Exemptions (Cal. Code Regs. tit. 14 § 15300)
Projects that are not expected to result in a significant environmental impact may be Categorically
Exempt from the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) review. As described in Cal. Code Regs.
tit. 14 § 15300, Section 21084 of the Public Resources Code requires these guidelines to include a list
of classes of projects which have been determined not to have a significant effect on the environment
and which shall, therefore, be exempt from the provisions of CEQA. In response to that mandate, the
Secretary for Resources has found that the following classes of projects listed in this article do not have
a significant effect on the environment, and they are declared to be categorically exempt from the
requirement for the preparation of environmental documents.

Like many campus-improvement projects, the proposed project is not expected to result in any
significant environmental impacts under CEQA. Table 1 provides an abbreviated CEQA checklist,




                                                                                                              117
                                                 Attachment 1


CEQA Notice of Exemption and Categorical Exemption Memorandum
James Cowan Fundamental Elementary School Site Security Project | Sacramento, California
March 10, 2026 | Terracon Project No. NB267030

explaining why the implementation of the proposed project would not result in any significant
environmental impacts.

Table 1. Abbreviated CEQA Environmental Checklist
 Impact Category        Description

                        The proposed project would not result in impacts related to scenic vistas or state-
                        listed scenic highways as the nearest scenic location is California State Route 160,
 Aesthetics             approximately 13 miles southwest of the school. Further, it would not require
                        the installation of equipment that would be inconsistent with previous
                        conditions; and would not result in an impact on Aesthetics.

 Agriculture and
                    The proposed project does not involve agricultural lands or Prime Farmland.
 Forestry Resources

                        The proposed project would not result in the exposure of pollutants or odors to
                        sensitive receptors. Although Sacramento County is in nonattainment status for
                        PM-2.5 (2006) and 8-Hour Ozone (2015) criteria pollutants, the proposed project
                        would not result in a significant net increase of such pollutants, nor would the
 Air Quality            project conflict with the Sacramento Metropolitan Air Quality Management
                                              ability to comply with any applicable State Implementation
                        Plan (SIP) as the emissions would be associated with the limited use of
                        construction equipment. The equipment required for this project would be
                        operated as needed for a limited amount of time.

                        Due to the developed and landscaped nature of the site, the project area is not
 Biological
                        expected to provide habitat for federally- or state-listed species, nor would it
 Resources
                        affect a Habitat Conservation Plan (HCP), or other sensitive biological resources.

                        The project area does not contain known historic properties and is not suspected
                        to contain archaeological artifacts as the general area is previously developed. If
 Cultural Resources     archaeological or cultural resources are discovered onsite, work would cease
                        immediately until consultation and approval to continue work was provided by a
                        qualified archaeologist.

                        The proposed project would not result in an impact related to the wasteful or
 Energy                 inefficient use of energy since the project will not require the use of equipment
                        with high energy requirements during its operations.

                        The proposed project would not result in geological impacts. The project
 Geology and Soils      construction would take place on previously developed land and would not result
                        in new construction in unsafe soils.

James Cowan Fundamental Elementary School Site Security Project | Sacramento, California
March 10, 2026 | Terracon Project No. NB267030


                        Although greenhouse gases would be emitted during project construction, it
 Greenhouse Gas
                        would not result in a significant impact on the environment, nor would it conflict
 Emissions
                        with a plan, policy, or regulation aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

                        The proposed project does not involve the use or release of potentially
 Hazards and
                        hazardous substances. If necessary, an Abatement of Hazardous Materials Plan
 Hazardous
                        (AHMP) will be created prior to project construction to handle potential
 Materials
                        unexpected encounters with hazardous materials.

                        Local water quality impacts would be avoided by the usage of routine
 Hydrology and
                        stormwater Best Management Practices (BMPs) during construction and
